text: Every morning, millions of people check the weather forecast before leaving home.
But how do experts predict what the sky will do?
The process begins with data collection.
Weather stations, satellites, and special balloons detect changes in temperature, wind, and air pressure.
This information is fed into powerful computers that use complex models to anticipate future conditions.
The computers generate predictions for the next few hours, days, and even weeks.
However, forecasts become less accurate the further ahead they look.
A five-day outlook is generally reliable, but anything beyond that is increasingly vague.
Scientists are constantly working to enhance these models by integrating new technology.
Despite the limitations, modern forecasting has undergone a tremendous transformation over the past few decades.
It saves lives by giving people sufficient time to prepare for storms and other severe weather events.
